opening_500: IN THE HIGH COURT OF JUSTICE
KING’S BENCH DIVISION
ADMINISTRATIVE COURT
Claim No: PA26-011500
BETWEEN:
BEN MAK
Claimant
— and —
WESTMINSTER CITY COUNCIL
Defendant
SKELETON ARGUMENT
Urgent Without-Notice Application — CPR 25.6 Interim Payment
KB Fees Certificate: 9 March 2026 | HWF Ref: HWF-23F-27E
SCHEDULE OF EXHIBITS: 12 CORE RECORDINGS
The institutional narrative, told in the words of the system itself.
A. INTRODUCTION
1.	A man told a public officer he was close to death. The officer replied:
“If you’re not dead, would you like the appointment on Monday or not.”

2.	That is recorded [BM-1]. It is verbatim. No crisis referral was made. No safeguarding alert was raised. The Claimant has been street homeless for approximately 505 days. Westminster’s own officer confirmed “Priority Need: YES” in writing [BM-3] and then provided nothing. This is an urgent without-notice application for an interim payment of £6,613.81 pursuant to CPR 25.6, arising from the Defendant’s failure to discharge its mandatory duty under s.188(1) Housing Act 1996.

3.	The Claimant seeks an interim payment of £6,613.81 in respect of hostel costs already incurred, evidenced by 319 bank-verified Monzo transactions [BM-RECEIPTS]. The legal basis is CPR 25.7(1)(c). The duty was confirmed in writing. The accommodation was never provided. The Claimant funded his own survival. Westminster still owes the money.

The duty was confirmed in writing. The accommodation was never provided.
The Claimant disclosed death. The officer offered Monday.
505 days homeless. 319 transactions. £6,613.81 bank-verified.
This application asks for 2.1% of the pleaded quantum. The duty is not in dispute.
B. LEGAL FRAMEWORK

4.	Section 188(1) Housing Act 1996: “If the local housing authority have reason to believe that an applicant may be homeless, eligible for assistance and have a priority need, they shall secure that accommodation is available for his occupation.” The word is “shall.” The Defendant’s own officer wrote “Priority Need: YES” [BM-3]. The Court need look no further than the Defendant’s own document.

5.	CPR 25.7(1)(c): The court may order an interim payment where satisfied that, if the claim went to trial, the claimant would obtain judgment for a substantial sum. The Scott Schedule [BM-6] quantifies the claim at £729,839+. The sum sought is £6,613.81 — 0.9% of the likely judgment. Westminster is a local authority; it cannot become insolvent. The payment is proportionate and safe.

6.	Article 3 ECHR: The absolute prohibition on inhuman or degrading treatment. In R (Limbuela) v SSHD [2005] UKHL 66, the House of Lords held that where a person is, by deliberate inaction of the state, denied shelter so as to be left destitute, Article 3 may be crossed. In MSS v Belgium and Greece (2011) 53 EHRR 2, the ECtHR held that failure to provide for a person in serious deprivation constituted degrading treatment. The Claimant has been street homeless for 505 days with a confirmed statutory duty undischarged. He disclosed imminent death. The officer asked about Monday.

7.	Section 15 Equality Act 2010: Discrimination arising from disability. The Claimant is autistic (diagnosed 11 April 2023, Dr Catherine Chung, CLAAS NHS Trust) [BM-8]. No reasonable adjustments were made to the assessment process. The Claimant cited s.20 from the street with greater precision than the officers paid to apply it. The failure was not ignorance. It was indifference.

8.	Misfeasance in Public Office: Malak Al-Mosawi confirmed “Priority Need: YES” in writing, provided nothing, and denied on a subsequent recording [BM-2] that Judy made the “If you’re not dead” statement. The original recording [BM-1] proves she did. Roger Muschett confirmed the Claimant’s circumstances on 26 September 2025 [BM-4] and took no action. Three officers. Thirty-two days. Each independently aware. Each independently inactive. The Court may properly draw the inference that this is institutional conduct.
C.	HOW THE CLAIMANT SPEAKS TO PEOPLE — AND HOW THEY SPEAK TO HIM
9.	Five exchanges define this case. Not as rhetoric — as recorded fact. Each one places the Claimant's words next to the system's words. The Court need not infer character. The Court can hear it.
Contrast 1: "I don't want you to get in trouble" — "If you're not dead"

10.	On 6 November 2025, a High Court officer told the Claimant that reasonable adjustments "aren't something that we can help with here" [BM-RA-CALL, 2:09]. The Claimant — homeless, disabled, suicidal, twice denied reasonable adjustments — responded at 2:19: "I want to correct you just because I don't want you to get in trouble. Reasonable adjustments — it's a legal mandate. So if a person has substantial difficulty then a reasonable adjustment must be honoured. So it's like saying if there was stairs and someone's in a wheelchair you couldn't turn them away." Westminster's response to the same man, on 29 August 2025, when he disclosed he was close to death [BM-1]: "If you're not dead, would you like the appointment on Monday or not." He protected the officer. The officer offered him Monday instead of safety.
Contrast 2: "She is a vulnerable woman — her needs are priority as mine" — "I'm not a PA, Ben"

11.	On 11 December 2025, while street homeless, the Claimant gave up his own court appointment in Farrer v Notting Hill Genesis (M04CL579) for a more vulnerable woman [BM-14]. HHJ Ashby commended his court papers in that case in open court [BM-13]. He sacrificed his hearing time for a stranger. On a separate occasion [1:14:45], when the Claimant asked Marilyn — his allocated support worker — for two bullet points summarising his position, a 30-second reasonable adjustment for an autistic person, her response: "I'm not a PA, Ben… I'm a supporter." He gave up his court time for someone else. His own worker would not give him thirty seconds of hers.
Contrast 3: "My feet are ruined" — "You look bloody good, though"

12.	At 41:02, the Claimant reports: "I've been upright and upright for nearly 48 hours. My feet are ruined because I never take my shoes off." At 41:16, Marilyn's response: "You know what, Ben? You look bloody good, though. Sorry to like. No, listen to Ben... One more thing... Could we get the name of the Bay Leaf company?" The body is broken. The worker compliments it. The suffering is disclosed. The worker pivots to an administrative question. At 1:16:24, the Claimant is in a state of extreme degradation. At 1:16:49, Marilyn claims he just needs to "exercise control." Under Article 3 ECHR, as applied in R (Limbuela) v SSHD [2005] UKHL 66, the response required by law is a safeguarding alert and emergency referral — not a compliment.
Contrast 4: "He is not sleeping rough — he is walking" — the Hotak violation

13.	Joanne MacWilliam's case note records the Claimant as "not sleeping rough" because he was "walking around." At 1:25:09, Joanne states: "I just find that you're very independent and you're able to do that yourself." Under Hotak v Southwark LBC [2015] UKSC 30, vulnerability must be compared to an ordinary person, not an ordinary homeless person. To use the Claimant's autistic autonomy — his ability to walk, to speak, to file proceedings — as evidence that he does not need help is to weaponise his disability against him. That is not an assessment. It is a trap.
Contrast 5: "Ben said he may be dead Monday" — the handover that never happened

14.	On 29 August 2025, the Claimant disclosed to Judy that he could be dead by Monday. Judy recorded it: "So just put it on your notes that then could be dead by Monday" [BM-1]. One hour later, Judy admitted: "I didn't send any notes. I just gave a general idea" [BM-2]. Malak Al-Mosawi, the next officer, denied the statement was ever made. Her explanation: she never had an efficient handover. The handover she claims she never received should have contained five words: "Ben said he may be dead Monday." The system heard a man say he might die. Its institutional response was to lose the information.

15.	Five contrasts. Five recordings. Five times the Claimant demonstrated more competence, more compassion, and more regard for the proper administration of justice than the officers and workers paid to deliver it. MPs write to him. Whistleblowers endorse him. Judges commend his papers. He cited statute from the street. He protected officers from the street. They could not discharge their duty from the office. Marilyn complimented his appearance while his feet were ruined. Joanne reclassified his homelessness as walking. The Court is respectfully invited to weigh that disparity.

WESTMINSTER CITY COUNCIL
GROUND 1 — BREACH OF SECTION 188(1) HOUSING ACT 1996

The Defendant’s attendance-based and telephone-based processes constitute a PCP placing the Claimant at substantial disadvantage.

8.	The Claimant is simultaneously conducting advocacy in Central London County Court (M04CL579), Liverpool Family Court (LV23C50471), and the Court of Appeal (CA-2024-001268) — demonstrating capability that is being undermined solely by the Defendant’s refusal to discharge its statutory duty.
WESTMINSTER CITY COUNCIL
PENAL NOTICE: If Westminster City Council disobeys this order they may be held in contempt of court and may be imprisoned, fined or have their assets seized.
